body_txt: Dogs. The oldest of the domesticated animals and man's constant companion throughout history. Brave, loyal, and friend to all. Except when they aren't . Hellhounds have been appearing since Hesiod wrote his Theogony , making this one Older Than Feudalism . Modern hellhounds typically resemble stereotypically aggressive or vicious dog breeds (such as bulldogs, Dobermans, or mastiffs) and are black with glowing red or flaming eyes. Less commonly, they may be black and white, or white with red ears. They may have two or even three heads. For the most part, hellhounds tend to be based on one of two sources. The first is within Scandinavian myth as wolves and other canine beasts that guard the realm of Hel. The second is a varied number of myths from the British Isles concerning spectral hounds said to roam fens, moors, ruins, and other abandoned places. Hellhounds of Norse pedigree tend to be guardians and watchdogs. Hellhounds of British pedigree are likelier to be lone wolves, roaming wildernesses on the prowl for hapless prey. They may possess additional supernatural powers but are usually less physically monstrous. As their name implies, hellhounds are generally thought to originate in Hell , The Underworld , or the local equivalent, but this has become a relaxed requirement for modern incarnations. Next to the hellhounds of Europe was another type of dog— Cerberus of Classical Mythology . A massive three-headed dog, Cerberus was tasked with guarding the Underworld from lingering souls. Unlike hellhounds, Cerberus was explicitly not a demon or ghost. In fact, it was a one of a kind entity. However, its reputation was so widespread that it ended up becoming A Kind of One and served as a model for many hellhound depictions thereafter likened to Cerberus and its associated traits such as multiple heads . Although hellhounds are known across Eurasia, the concept of a supernatural hound is nothing new. Indeed, multiple civilizations viewed The Underworld differently from Hell ; the former had otherworldly canines long before hellhounds were conceived, and they were nothing like hellhounds, being neither demonic nor malevolent, nor a glorified guard dog. A major example was the jackal-headed Psychopomp Anubis, the Egyptian god of the dead, who paved the way for dogs being associated with the afterlife. Because of this, not every supernatural canine, especially those of the underworld, is necessarily a hellhound. With modern day erroneously conflating most depictions of The Underworld for the theological Hell, however, it's not uncommon to see Egyptian and other myths given demonic undertones to bring them closer to the fiery Satanic Archetype that hellhounds tend to embody. Although their origins are impossibly varied, hellhounds can generally be lumped into three categories: Hunter Escaped or deliberately released from Hell , these hellhounds exist only to hunt and kill. These are usually "hellhound classic," appearing as black hounds with red eyes. The eponymous hound from The Hound of the Baskervilles is probably the most famous example of this type, although it turned out to be a fake . Guardian This version is usually just as dangerous as the Hunter, but it is tasked with guarding a location or person. If they're guarding a place, it's usually an entrance to Hell or the Underworld. If they're guarding a person, that person is usually associated with Hell. Garmr, the Norse equivalent to the Greek Cerberus, is the guard dog to the original Hel. Portent The sight of one of these black dogs was a Portent of Doom . They might be malevolent or outright dangerous. Myths are split between the sight of the Black Dog being the cause of the misfortune or merely a symptom. The Barghest of Yorkshire may be the best known example, although the Grim from Harry Potter may be replacing it due to Pop-Cultural Osmosis . Classical hellhounds are immune to Kick the Dog , and their obvious menace makes their counterattack less a case of The Dog Bites Back than a result of Bullying a Dragon . It's almost impossible to scare these dogs , so any character who can do it is one to be very cautious around. May occasionally overlap with Post-Apocalyptic Dog and/or Savage Wolves , but hellhounds are generally more evil and supernatural. Even if the Cats Are Mean , they are still horrified by these kinds of dogs (the feline equivalent would be cat-sith or other equivalent hellcats). Hellhounds may be part of The Wild Hunt or the Legions of Hell . Compare to Hellish Horse and Scavengers Are Scum . Contrast Asian Lion Dogs , Big Friendly Dog and Heroic Dog . See also Inugami , a malevolent Japanese hound. May be a Sub-Trope of Ghostly Animals depending on the exact nature of the hellhound in question.
